planting.note: Direct sow in early spring before last frost in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 4–9; Mitsuba (Japanese Honewort, Japanese Parsley) is a hardy shade herb native to the cool, moist woodlands of Japan, China, and Korea — with a distinctive, delicate flavor combining notes of parsley, celery, and angelica with a hint of anise — one of the most important fresh garnishing herbs in Japanese cuisine, added raw to clear soups, salads, tempura, sukiyaki, and sashimi.

directions: Direct sow Cryptotaenia japonica in cool weather in moist, humus-rich soil in part to deep shade; keep evenly moist and succession-sow every 3–4 weeks for continuous harvest; harvest individual leaves and stems as needed, or cut entire plants to 2 inches above the crown for regrowth; plants self-sow readily in favorable conditions. Mitsuba (Japanese for 'three leaves', referring to its three-leaflet compound leaves) is an essential herb in Japanese cooking — used fresh, never cooked at length — as a garnish and subtle flavoring in osuimono (clear soup), chawanmushi (savory egg custard), nabemono (hot pot), and as a fresh addition to salads and scattered over finished dishes. The flavor is uniquely delicate: more complex than parsley, less anise-forward than chervil, with a clean, fresh quality that bridges East Asian and Western herb traditions. Unlike most herbs that demand full sun, Mitsuba thrives in the deep shade of a woodland garden or the north side of a building — making it one of the most valuable herbs for shaded kitchen gardens. In zone 7 and warmer it can be semi-evergreen; in zone 4–6 it dies back to the root in winter but regrows reliably in spring. The plant self-sows freely in suitable conditions and can naturalize under trees or along shaded borders. Etiolated (blanched) stems grown in darkness are particularly prized for their milder, more delicate flavor.
sources: Japan Agricultural Cooperatives (JA) — mitsuba cultivation guide
Shizuoka Prefectural Agriculture Information — Cryptotaenia japonica

subcategory: North American native anise-flavored shade herb

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall (they require cold stratification — naturally satisfied by winter cold) in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral forest soil in zone 3–8; Smooth Sweet Cicely is a graceful native woodland herb of North America — closely related to the European Sweet Cicely (Myrrhis odorata) — with a distinctive, pervasive anise-licorice fragrance throughout the plant and edible roots, young shoots, and seeds used by Native Americans for centuries as food, flavoring, and medicine.

directions: Sow Osmorhiza longistylis seeds in fall directly in the garden — seeds are slow to germinate and require cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify in damp paper towels in the refrigerator for 60–90 days before spring sowing; plants self-sow freely once established and can naturalize into a fragrant woodland groundcover. Smooth Sweet Cicely is an elegant, airy, native woodland herb that fills the shaded understory with its finely divided, carrot-family foliage and pervasive anise fragrance throughout the growing season — the roots especially smell strongly of sweet anise when bruised. All parts are edible: young spring leaves used as a mild anise flavoring in salads, fish dishes, and desserts; the thick, carrot-like taproot grated or eaten raw for its strong anise flavor; the immature green seeds used as a spice. Multiple Indigenous nations used Osmorhiza roots as food and medicine — the Cheyenne as a cold remedy, the Ojibwe as an eye wash, and the Iroquois as a root vegetable cooked with meat. The white, flat-topped flower clusters attract numerous small native bees, wasps, and flies. An excellent companion for ferns, wild ginger, and trilliums in naturalistic woodland gardens; once established, it requires no care beyond controlling spread if self-sowing becomes excessive.
sources: Native American Ethnobotany Database — Osmorhiza longistylis
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Osmorhiza longistylis

subcategory: European heirloom root vegetable cool season

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall — they require cold stratification and germinate in early spring — in partial shade in moist, deep, well-drained, fertile, sandy loam in zone 5–9; Turnip-rooted Chervil (Bulbous Chervil) is a rare heirloom root vegetable of European origin bearing small, turnip-shaped tubers with grey-brown skin and creamy-white, nutty, chestnut-flavored flesh — once popular in 19th-century French and German kitchen gardens but nearly lost to cultivation, now enjoying renewed interest among heirloom vegetable enthusiasts and gourmet chefs.

directions: Sow Chaerophyllum bulbosum seeds fresh in fall directly where they are to grow — seeds lose viability quickly and are best sown immediately after harvest; thin seedlings to 4–6 inches apart and keep soil moist but not waterlogged throughout the cool growing season; harvest roots in early to midsummer as foliage yellows and dies back. Turnip-rooted Chervil is among the rarest and most intriguing of all heirloom root vegetables — an annual or biennial carrot-family plant with delicate, ferny foliage and small, grey-skinned tubers with exceptionally delicate, nutty, chesnut-sweet flavor. It requires cold stratification to germinate (hence fall sowing is essential) and tolerates partial shade well — performing in spots where carrots or parsnips would not thrive. The roots are at their best when small, around an inch in diameter; larger roots can become pithy. Their flavor has been compared to a cross between chestnut, sweet potato, and parsley root — distinctive and prized by those lucky enough to taste them. Auguste Escoffier featured turnip-rooted chervil in classic French cuisine; it fell from fashion in the 20th century as it does not store or ship well and demands specific growing conditions. Fresh seeds from specialty heirloom seed suppliers are essential as germination rates drop sharply after a few months. The feathery foliage is also edible and mildly anise-flavored, usable as a garnish like regular chervil.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall in partial shade in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist woodland or hedgerow soil in zone 5–9; Pignut (Earth Chestnut, Earthnut) is a slender, delicate, native British and European woodland herb — once eaten by children and common folk throughout Britain for its small, brown-skinned, sweet, nutty-flavored underground tubers — bearing finely divided, carrot-family foliage in hedgerows, meadows, and oak and beech woodland understories, and an important food plant for dormice, wild boar, and other woodland wildlife.

directions: Sow Conopodium majus seeds in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; plants are slow to establish and the small underground tubers take 2–3 years to reach harvestable size; avoid disturbing the soil around plants once growing; harvest small tubers with a trowel in summer as foliage dies back. Pignut is one of the most charming native edibles of British and European woodlands — a slender, graceful plant of meadows, hedgerows, and open woodland that has been gathered for its sweet, nut-flavored tubers by country people for centuries. The small, round to irregularly shaped tubers (technically a corm-like swelling of the underground stem) are covered in brown skin and have cream-colored, very sweet, crunchy flesh with a flavor reminiscent of sweet chestnut or hazelnut — edible raw or cooked. Foragers prize them but they are difficult to dig without following the thin, brittle stem underground without breaking it (hence their reputation for elusiveness). Wild populations are protected in Britain, but cultivation from seed is permitted and encouraged for those wishing to grow them. The plant is an important food source for dormice, wild boar, and other wildlife in European woodland ecosystems. A genuinely rare and unusual woodland garden plant with culinary value and strong ecological connections.
sources: Plants for a Future — Conopodium majus

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained woodland soil in zone 4–8; Fairy Lanterns (Hooker's Fairy Bells) is a graceful, rhizomatous woodland perennial native to the shaded coniferous and mixed forests of the Pacific Coast from British Columbia to central California — producing arching, leafy stems and pairs of small, nodding, cream-colored bells and bright-red-orange berries that glow like lanterns in the understory shade.

directions: Plant Prosartes hookeri in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade under conifers or deciduous trees; mulch with leaf mold or bark to maintain cool, moist, acidic conditions; the plant spreads slowly from rhizomes to form attractive colonies — divide every 3–4 years if desired. Fairy Lanterns is a beautiful native woodland perennial of the Pacific Coast forests — closely related to and often still listed as Disporum hookeri — forming elegant clumps of arching, leafy stems that emerge in early spring and bear pairs of small, narrow, nodding, creamy-white bell-flowers at the branch tips. The common name comes from the brilliant red-orange berries that follow the flowers in late summer, glowing like tiny lanterns in the deep forest shade. The plant grows naturally in the humus-rich, acidic, perpetually moist soil of Pacific Coast conifer forests alongside Trillium, Oxalis, and ferns; in cultivation it prefers analogous conditions — reliable moisture, acidic soil, and overhead canopy shade. A refined and overlooked native plant that deserves wider use in Pacific Northwest woodland gardens and shady borders; it pairs beautifully with native ferns, bleeding heart (Dicentra formosa), and wild ginger (Asarum caudatum). The berries are not considered edible for humans but are taken by thrushes and other woodland birds.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Prosartes hookeri

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in bright partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–11; Ceratozamia robusta is a large, impressive cycad endemic to the tropical rainforests of Veracruz and Oaxaca, Mexico — producing a stout trunk with long, arching, pinnate fronds bearing many pairs of broad, glossy, dark-green leaflets — one of the most ornamental of the Ceratozamia genus, and threatened in the wild by habitat loss and illegal collection.

directions: Plant Ceratozamia robusta in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-enriched soil in bright partial shade; protect from frost below 28°F, direct summer sun (which bleaches the glossy fronds), and waterlogged conditions; fertilize with a slow-release cycad fertilizer containing trace elements twice yearly in spring and summer. Caution: all parts of Ceratozamia robusta are toxic — seeds, leaves, and roots contain cycasin — keep away from children and livestock. Robust Horncone is one of the largest and most impressive species in the genus Ceratozamia — a Mexican cycad group distinguished from other New World cycads by the paired 'horns' on the tops of male and female cone scales. The species is native to humid tropical and montane forests of Veracruz and Oaxaca at elevations from near sea level to about 1,500 meters, where it grows as a forest understory plant in deep, humus-rich soil. Long, arching pinnate fronds (each 4–6 feet in length) bearing numerous pairs of broad, glossy dark-green leaflets create a dramatic, palm-like effect in the shade garden. Growth is very slow; a cone-bearing adult specimen represents many decades of cultivation and is a highly prized collector's plant. Ceratozamia robusta is listed on CITES Appendix I (the most restrictive protection level) due to severe wild population declines from habitat clearing and poaching; purchase only from reputable nurseries with documentation of nursery origin.
sources: Cycad Society of South Africa — Ceratozamia robusta
